text: Hello and welcome back.
I am Dr. Shivanshu Mishra, Advanced Laparoscopic, Hernia and Bariatric Surgeon, Shivani Hospital, Kanpur.
Today I am going to present laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
This is a twenty six year old lady who presented to us with symptomatic calculus cholecystitis.
She had no previous history of comorbidities or any previous surgery.

The patient is positioned head-up for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The first port is placed at the 10mm supraumbilical region, followed by a 5mm to 10mm trocar at the xiphoid process. Additional 5mm ports are positioned at the mid-clavicular line and mid-axillary line.
Upon entering the abdomen, the laparoscopic view reveals a distended gallbladder.
The surgeon begins tracing the common bile duct (CBD) in the laparoscopic view, preparing for further dissection during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the fatty calloused angle of the gallbladder, which is distended and symptomatic of calculus cholecystitis.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to apply gentle bursts to the tissue, likely for dissection or cauterization purposes.
The surgeon begins the posterior dissection of the gallbladder, initiating the procedure by carefully separating the posterior tissue layers.
The surgeon releases the peritoneum posteriorly, revealing the underlying anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction on the gallbladder, pulling it towards the right and lateral side to facilitate exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon delineates the cystic duct, making it visually distinct from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully performs posterior dissection using electrocautery, emphasizing precision in separating tissue layers behind the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses bursts of coagulation to gently release posterior adhesions, visible in the surgical field.
Never use continuous cautery over the cystic duct or any vital structures at the carrot's triangle.
The surgeon attempts to create a window around the cystic duct, using careful dissection techniques to separate it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon continues dissection upwards along the cystic duct, aiming to delineate the cystic artery. The importance of careful posterior dissection is emphasized, ensuring clear visualization of these structures.
The surgeon clearly visualizes the cystic duct and artery, preparing to create a window between these vital structures within the surgical field.
The cystic artery comes into view, followed by the artery to the cystic duct. The surgeon gently coagulates the artery to the cystic duct using bursts of coagulation.
The surgeon milks the cystic duct, applying gentle pressure to clear its contents before placing clips on the duct.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic duct and artery with the liver visible between these structures.
The surgeon applies endoclips to the cystic duct, placing a primary clip, a secondary clip, and a sentinel clip, before cutting the duct between the clips.
The surgeon uses a bipolar energy source to coagulate the cystic artery, which is an end artery, ensuring hemostasis before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon uses clips on the cystic duct and artery, while also employing a bipolar energy source for coagulation, ensuring a safe procedure.
The surgeon uses a hook to remove the gallbladder from the liver bed after addressing the cystic artery and duct, with the gallbladder visibly separating from the liver tissue.
See how important is the traction and counter traction in this aspect.
The surgeon demonstrates the risk of bleeding when dissecting deeply into the liver bed during gallbladder removal.
The surgeon gently navigates to find the avascular plane between the gallbladder and liver bed, minimizing bleeding during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon maneuvers the gallbladder back and forth, gently repositioning the instruments to cut within an avascular plane, minimizing bleeding during the dissection.
This is how we avoid unnecessary burning of the liver and unnecessary bleeding.
And this can be done very gently and very beautifully without any blood loss.
The gallbladder is fully excised and is being removed through the epigastric tenement port.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding in the surgical field following the removal of the gallbladder.
The surgical field is irrigated with saline solution, providing a local wash to clear debris and improve visibility.
The surgical field is shown post-cholecystectomy, with minimal bleeding visible at the operated site.
The surgical procedure is completed, with the gallbladder specimen removed through the epigastric tenement port, and the surgical site showing minimal bleeding.
Thank you for watching.
Kindly do like, share and subscribe.
